A PROPOSAL FOR A long weekend · 18–22 June 2026

José in London - Chapter II.
A solstice at
the Stones

THU 18 JUN → MON 22 JUN · LONDON → STONEHENGE

Our weekend runs from candlelight to the most special sunrise of the year. I think it's one of the best collections of London by night, a day of art and movement, and a Summer Solstice at Stonehenge to close it out.

London culture Food & music Friends Art Climbing Ancient Britain The solstice
Thursday 18 June

Arrival & London by night

Land, hop on train and meet Sinzi at Temple ♥️
Westminster Abbey, ramen, and a jazz night.

Locked
17:00

Evensong at Westminster Abbey

Arrive 16:20–16:30; entry is free for those coming to worship rather than to sightsee. A genuinely good way to land quietly after travel.

Evensong is the sung form of Evening Prayer – the office Cranmer distilled from the older monastic hours of Vespers and Compline and set into English. The choir carries most of it; you mainly listen. Its two fixed canticles will be familiar to you as a Catholic – the Magnificat and the Nunc Dimittis – here in Tudor English, sung by a choir that has been refining these settings for centuries.

For the curious

A church has stood on this spot since around 960; the Gothic building you'll sit in was begun by Henry III in 1245 – so it's roughly 780 years old, and it ran as a Catholic monastery for its first 570-odd years, until the break with Rome in the 1530s. The short history of that break, and where the two churches still part ways for you as a Catholic, is in the annex.

Read the annex ↓
After

Ramen

Somewhere central, easy reach of Soho. Location TBD/TBC.

20:30

Jahari Stampley Soul Family Trio at Ronnie Scott's

Main room – your first Ronnie Scott's (my second), and – bonus one!!! – the first time you meet some London friends, with Stefano and Ana joining us.

▶ Watch the trio liveJahari Stampley · reference clip – swap for the night's set
Worth knowing

Open since 1959, it's the oldest jazz club in the country – Miles Davis, Ella Fitzgerald, Nina Simone, Chet Baker and Amy Winehouse all played this room, and Jimi Hendrix gave his last-ever public performance here. The short history is in the annex.

Read the annex ↓

The evening walks itself: Abbey → ramen → jazz → friends → home & cuddling.

Thursday alternatives
B

St Paul's evensong

Spectacular interior, similar musical experience – slightly less intimate than Westminster.

C

South Bank evening

Riverside walk, drinks, dinner. The low-pressure arrival day if travel runs long.

Friday 19 June

Half work, some big art & some climbing

A half-day at the desk, then pigment and walls – Anish Kapoor's already booked, and the evening's a choice between more looking or some doing.

Locked
Morning

Breakfast at the café, then WFH

The English Breakfast + flowers

After work

Anish Kapoor at the Hayward Gallery

Tickets already booked. One of the major London shows of summer 2026. Really, really special thing to see.

How to shape the evening
A

Art-focused

Kapoor → National Gallery → South Bank walk → dinner & drinks. A strong, relaxed cultural day with easy logistics.

B

Art + climbing

Kapoor → National Gallery (only if it isn't too much art for one day) → climbing → dinner after. More variety, better balance between looking and doing.

SINZI To-dO REMINDERAsk Carol's National Gallery membership card before Friday.
Climbing venue – pick by priority
1

The Castle Climbing Centre

Pros

The most memorable venue – a beautiful historic building that looks like a castle (shockingly, I know)

My favourite one

Get to experience a bit of North London, where the people are a bit nicer

Cons

Furthest to travel though (30min from Central London, 1h to Chiswick)

Walls not as big as Westway (where we went last time)

If the venue itself is the point
2

The Font, Wandsworth

Best logistics, convenient from central London. Less iconic, but very modern, clean and neat (too much to some extent). Also, walls not that big.

If convenience wins
3

Westway Climbing Centre

You've been here before. The Lead Mecca. Bigger walls, more extensive climbing. Least novelty for me because it is the one I climb most often.

If the climbing itself is the priority
Saturday 20 June

The run-up to the stones

Fixed objective: be at Stonehenge overnight for Sunday's sunrise. Three ways to get there, in order of preference.

Option 2 · Preferred

London half-day + rental car

Daytime, option 1: hop-on-hop-off bus, central sightseeing, markets – a relaxed London day.
Daytime, option 2: take the bikes out to Richmond for a ride. See the deer. Spend some time outdoors. Maybe visit Richmond - the town. Eat around Chiswick. Take a long nap before the night.

Early evening: back to Chiswick to rest. Around midnight: collect the car, drive to Stonehenge. Sunday: sunrise, explore nearby sites, drive back.

For

Maximum flexibility

Extra London time

Easy to add nearby sites

Against

Driving sleep-deprived

More moving parts

Option 3 · Wildcard

Make it a bike trip

The idea: keep the original plan open and actually give cycling a go. The catch is fit – the only bike ready to test is mine, probably a few sizes down from your size, but maybe manageable.
An easy daytime loop – Chiswick → Richmond → Richmond Park → coffee → back – will tell us after an hour or two whether it's rideable for the weekend. If it isn't, fall back to the car or a train/shuttle.

Priority order, so this stays a side-quest
  1. The Stonehenge experience
  2. Sleep and energy
  3. Simple logistics
  4. Cycling, if it's actually comfortable
Option 1

Salisbury base

Daytime: train to Salisbury – cathedral, old-town walk, pub lunch, early dinner, then a hotel nap (only if very affordable). Late evening: shuttle to Stonehenge. Sunday morning: sunrise, shuttle back, breakfast, return to London.

For

Lowest stress

No driving

Room to nap and recover

Most comfortable

Against

Less adventurous

Sunday 21 June

Summer solstice

The Zenith of the sun, the climax of the weekend..

Locked
~4:52am

First light at Stonehenge

The sun comes up behind the Heel Stone and runs straight down the monument's axis into the circle – the alignment the whole thing was built to frame. The main event of the weekend.

The good stuff

What actually happens at the solstice, who raised these stones five thousand years ago, the druids and the pagan side of it, and the other ancient sites a short hop away – Woodhenge, Durrington Walls, Amesbury – are all gathered in the annex.

Read the annex ↓
Monday 22 June

Half a day, and goodbye :(

Just be at the house.

Locked
Morning

Breakfast at home & chill

Nowhere to be. Coffee, the last of the weekend, no rushing.

13:45

Flight home

And that's Chapter II. Until the next one.

The shortlist

Current winning version

Thursday
Westminster Abbey evensong → ramen → Ronnie Scott's with friends.
Friday
Flower breakfast → WFH → Anish Kapoor → National Gallery → climbing (Castle preferred) → dinner.
Saturday
Salisbury or London day depending on logistics → rest → Stonehenge overnight.
Sunday
Stonehenge sunrise → Woodhenge / Durrington Walls / Amesbury → back to London.
Monday
Breakfast at home → a slow morning → flight at 13:45.

It threads together spiritual Britain, London culture, friends, contemporary art, physical activity, ancient Britain, and the solstice – without cramming any single day.

Annex · the good stuff

Things worth knowing, the longer way round

The English church, and where it parts from Rome

The Abbey began as a Benedictine monastery founded around 960, under King Edgar and St Dunstan. Edward the Confessor rebuilt it in stone – his church was consecrated in 1065 – and Henry III pulled that down and began the soaring Gothic building you'll sit in on 6 July 1245. It has been the coronation church since 1066, holds some 3,300 burials, and was Catholic, like every English church, for its first 570-odd years. The break came in the 1530s, when Henry VIII, refused an annulment by the Pope, had Parliament declare the monarch rather than the Bishop of Rome head of the Church in England (Act of Supremacy, 1534). Edward VI pushed it Protestant; Mary I briefly restored Rome; Elizabeth I settled it in 1559 as a deliberate via media – a church that kept bishops, liturgy and sacraments but answered to the Crown, not the papacy. The monarch is still its Supreme Governor (Elizabeth softened Henry's "Supreme Head," on the logic that Christ alone heads the Church). The Abbey itself is a "Royal Peculiar" – answerable directly to the sovereign, not to any bishop.

Where it diverges from Catholicism
Authority
No Pope; Scripture is supreme, with tradition and reason alongside. Catholicism adds the living Magisterium and papal teaching authority.
The Eucharist
Anglicans affirm Christ's "real presence," but the Thirty-Nine Articles reject transubstantiation as the explanation; Catholic doctrine affirms it.
Saints & purgatory
Pared back or set aside at the Reformation; central or affirmed in Catholic practice.
Mary
Honoured, but the Immaculate Conception and the Assumption aren't binding dogma as they are for Catholics.
Clergy
Anglican priests may marry, and the CofE ordains women as priests (1994) and bishops (2014); the Roman priesthood is male and, in the Latin rite, celibate.
Sacraments
Anglicanism foregrounds the two it holds were given by Christ – baptism and communion; Catholicism numbers seven.

One sharp point for you: Rome has never recognised the validity of Anglican holy orders – Leo XIII called them "absolutely null and utterly void" in 1896 – so from the Vatican's side the priest leading worship isn't ordained at all. And Anglicanism spans a wide range, from Anglo-Catholics whose worship feels a half-step from Rome to low-church evangelicals who'd find the Abbey's incense and polyphony foreign.

The connective tissue is real, though: the creeds, the Scriptures, baptism, the shape of the liturgy, and the two Evensong canticles are all common ground – which is why, at Evensong, you'll recognise far more than feels foreign.

Ronnie Scott's

Two saxophonists, Ronnie Scott and Pete King, opened the club in a Soho basement on Gerrard Street on 30 October 1959, then moved it to 47 Frith Street – where you'll be – in 1965. It was the first British club to bring American jazz greats over to play regularly, and the guest list since reads like the genre's hall of fame: Miles Davis, Ella Fitzgerald, Nina Simone, Sarah Vaughan, Chet Baker, Sonny Rollins. Scott himself ran the room as compère, famous for a deadpan stream of terrible jokes between sets. Amy Winehouse, the great Soho voice of her generation, sang here too – the club runs all the way through her story, and through the Back to Black film about her. Its sharpest footnote: Jimi Hendrix played his last-ever public performance here, sitting in with Eric Burdon's band a few days before his death in September 1970. It's now the oldest jazz club in the country, and the Frith Street room is deliberately tiny – low light, close tables, the band almost in your lap.

Stonehenge & the solstice

The stones went up in stages from around 3000 BC, with the great sarsen circle raised about 2500 BC – roughly five thousand years ago, older than the Pyramids' completion, by people who left no writing. Whatever else it was – temple, calendar, burial ground, healing site – it was built to catch the sun: the monument's axis lines up with sunrise at the summer solstice and sunset at the winter one. On the longest morning, the sun climbs the north-east horizon directly behind the outlying Heel Stone and throws its first light straight into the heart of the circle.

English Heritage, which looks after the site, opens it for free "Managed Open Access" across solstice night – one of the very few times a year anyone can walk in among the stones, which are otherwise roped off. Tens of thousands come. People arrive in the dark from around 1–3am; druids in white robes and modern pagans hold blessings and chants, drums go all night, and at sunrise (about 4:52am in 2026) the whole field turns to face the Heel Stone. It's part ancient rite, part all-night festival – and even in a crowd, the moment the light lands is genuinely moving. Dress warm: Salisbury Plain is cold before dawn even in June.

The sites around Stonehenge

Woodhenge
A Neolithic timber monument a couple of miles north-east – once concentric rings of huge wooden posts, now marked by concrete markers, contemporary with Stonehenge itself.
Durrington Walls
A vast henge enclosure beside Woodhenge, thought to be the settlement where the people who built and used Stonehenge actually lived and feasted.
Amesbury
The historic town right next door – claimed as one of the longest continuously occupied settlements in Britain, and a natural stop for food and a breather.
